ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 covers clam as a general category; no Singapore-specific lala (Paphia undulata / Meretrix spp.) entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers clams and cockles as a group. No species-specific lala entry exists in FoodEx2; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144007 covers clam as a food substance. No lala-specific code exists; closest parent used.

main_ingredients: lala clams (Paphia undulata or Meretrix spp.)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of lala clams in Singapore is approximately 300 g (including shells), equivalent to roughly 150 g edible clam meat with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Clams, mixed species, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174202)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy raw clam data (mixed species), which is the closest approved source for lala clams (Paphia undulata / Meretrix spp.). Lala are small short-neck clams common in Singapore and Malaysia; their nutritional profile is closely comparable to USDA mixed-species clam data. Values represent edible meat per 100 g. Sodium is elevated slightly to reflect typical stir-fried restaurant preparation with soy sauce and oyster sauce. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated lala entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-quality approved fallback.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lala clams specifically. Shellfish and lean seafood are universally classified as very low GI foods due to negligible carbohydrate content (2.6 g per 100 g edible meat). GI is estimated at 5, consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of shellfish and lean protein foods as low GI (GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving (approx. 150 g edible meat) = (5 × 3.9 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.2. This is consistent with peer-reviewed GI literature for bivalve molluscs.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from restaurant preparation with soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with restaurant-prepared lala clams, as stir-frying with soy sauce and oyster sauce can raise sodium content to approximately 480 mg per 100 g edible meat; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or limiting portion size is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lala clams are an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, providing approximately 12.8 g protein and only 2.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g edible meat, resulting in a negligible glycemic load and minimal postprandial glucose impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, and high vitamin B12
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lala clams are cardioprotective in their natural form due to low saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g), beneficial polyunsaturated fatty acids, and exceptionally high vitamin B12 (49.4 mcg per 100 g); however, the high sodium content of restaurant preparations warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lala clams (蛤蜊) are small short-neck bivalve molluscs, typically of the species Paphia undulata or Meretrix spp., widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, and Southeast Asia. In Singapore, lala is a beloved seafood dish commonly served at Chinese seafood restaurants, zi char stalls, and hawker centres. The clams are typically stir-fried in a wok over high heat with garlic, ginger, chilli, oyster sauce, and soy sauce, producing a fragrant, savoury dish with a slightly briny, umami-rich flavour. The clam meat is tender and slightly chewy with a clean oceanic taste. Lala can also be prepared steamed, BBQ-style, or in a spicy sambal sauce. Nutritionally, lala clams are an excellent lean protein source (approximately 12.8 g protein per 100 g edible meat) with very low fat (1.0 g) and low carbohydrate (2.6 g), making them suitable for low-carbohydrate and high-protein dietary patterns. They are exceptionally rich in vitamin B12 (49.4 mcg per 100 g, far exceeding daily requirements), iron (13.98 mg per 100 g), selenium, and potassium. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI 5), making lala clams appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical concern is sodium content, which increases substantially in restaurant preparations using soy sauce and oyster sauce. Lala is not halal-certified in most Singapore contexts due to the nature of shellfish classification in Islamic dietary law. The dish is a staple of Chinese seafood cuisine in Singapore and is frequently ordered as a shared dish at the table.
common_user_queries: Are lala clams high in cholesterol?
meal_context: shared seafood dish at Chinese restaurant

confidence_reason: No dedicated lala clam entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy mixed-species raw clam data (FDC ID 174202), which is the highest-quality approved source available for this food. The adaptation is defensible given the close biological similarity between lala and USDA mixed-species clams. Sodium is adjusted upward to reflect typical Singapore restaurant preparation. GI is an analogous estimate consistent with peer-reviewed shellfish GI literature.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy clam entry (FDC ID 174202). 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed as negligible/trace in USDA SR Legacy data for raw clams. 3. Sugar is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for raw clams. 4. Dietary fibre is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for raw clam meat. 5. Sodium value of 480 mg per 100 g reflects adapted restaurant-preparation estimate incorporating soy sauce and oyster sauce; raw USDA clam sodium is approximately 56 mg per 100 g. 6. Halal status is false as lala clams (shellfish/molluscs) are generally not considered halal under Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence predominant in Singapore and Malaysia, though some schools differ. 7. GI set to 5 as an analogous estimate for shellfish/bivalve molluscs consistent with University of Sydney GI Database classification of lean seafood as very low GI. 8. The cuisine_tag 'Crab' in the input record appears to be a platform tagging artefact and has not been carried forward as it does not describe this food.
